title: Diffusion-Based Voice Conversion with Fast Maximum Likelihood Sampling Scheme

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## DIFFUSION-BASED VOICE CONVERSION WITH FAST MAXIMUM LIKELIHOOD SAMPLING SCHEME

## **Vadim Popov, Ivan Vovk, Vladimir Gogoryan,**

Huawei Noah’s Ark Lab, Moscow, Russia

_{_ vadim.popov,vovk.ivan,gogoryan.vladimir _}_ @huawei.com

## **Tasnima Sadekova, Mikhail Kudinov & Jiansheng Wei**

Huawei Noah’s Ark Lab, Moscow, Russia

_{_ sadekova.tasnima,kudinov.mikhail,weijiansheng _}_ @huawei.com

## ABSTRACT

Voice conversion is a common speech synthesis task which can be solved in different ways depending on a particular real-world scenario. The most challenging one often referred to as one-shot many-to-many voice conversion consists in copying target voice from only one reference utterance in the most general case when both source and target speakers do not belong to the training dataset. We present a scalable high-quality solution based on diffusion probabilistic modeling and demonstrate its superior quality compared to state-of-the-art one-shot voice conversion approaches. Moreover, focusing on real-time applications, we investigate general principles which can make diffusion models faster while keeping synthesis quality at a high level. As a result, we develop a novel Stochastic Differential Equations solver suitable for various diffusion model types and generative tasks as shown through empirical studies and justify it by theoretical analysis.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Voice conversion (VC) is the task of copying the target speaker’s voice while preserving the linguistic content of the utterance pronounced by the source speaker. Practical VC applications often require a model which is able to operate in one-shot mode (i.e. when only one reference utterance is provided to copy the target speaker’s voice) for any source and target speakers. Such models are usually referred to as one-shot many-to-many models (or sometimes zero-shot many-to-many models, or just any-to-any VC models). It is challenging to build such a model since it should be able to adapt to a new unseen voice having only one spoken utterance pronounced with it, so it was not until recently that successful one-shot VC solutions started to appear.

Conventional one-shot VC models are designed as autoencoders whose latent space ideally contains only the linguistic content of the encoded utterance while target voice identity information (usually taking shape of speaker embedding) is fed to the decoder as conditioning. Whereas in the pioneering AutoVC model (Qian et al., 2019) only speaker embedding from the pre-trained speaker verification network was used as conditioning, several other models improved on AutoVC enriching conditioning with phonetic features such as pitch and loudness (Qian et al., 2020; Nercessian, 2020), or training voice conversion and speaker embedding networks jointly (Chou & Lee, 2019). Also, several papers (Lin et al., 2021; Ishihara & Saito, 2020; Liu et al., 2021b) made use of attention mechanism to better fuse specific features of the reference utterance into the source utterance thus improving the decoder performance. Apart from providing the decoder with sufficiently rich information, one of the main problems autoencoder VC models face is to disentangle source speaker identity from speech content in the encoder. Some models (Qian et al., 2019; 2020; Nercessian, 2020) solve this problem by introducing an information bottleneck. Among other popular solutions of the disentanglement problem one can mention applying vector quantization technique to the content information (Wu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021), utilizing features of Variational AutoEncoders (Luong & Tran, 2021; Saito et al., 2018; Chou & Lee, 2019), introducing instance normalization layers (Chou & Lee, 2019; Chen et al., 2021b), and using Phonetic Posteriorgrams (PPGs) (Nercessian, 2020; Liu et al., 2021b).

1

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

The model we propose in this paper solves the disentanglement problem by employing the encoder predicting “average voice”: it is trained to transform mel features corresponding to each phoneme into mel features corresponding to this phoneme averaged across a large multi-speaker dataset. As for decoder, in our VC model, it is designed as a part of a Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DPM) since this class of generative models has shown very good results in speech-related tasks like raw waveform generation (Chen et al., 2021a; Kong et al., 2021) and mel feature generation (Popov et al., 2021; Jeong et al., 2021). However, this decoder choice poses a problem of slow inference because DPM forward pass scheme is iterative and to obtain high-quality results it is typically necessary to run it for hundreds of iterations (Ho et al., 2020; Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021). Addressing this issue, we develop a novel inference scheme that significantly reduces the number of iterations sufficient to produce samples of decent quality and does not require model re-training. Although several attempts have been recently made to reduce the number of DPM inference steps (Song et al., 2021a; San-Roman et al., 2021; Watson et al., 2021; Kong & Ping, 2021; Chen et al., 2021a), most of them apply to some particular types of DPMs. In contrast, our approach generalizes to all popular kinds of DPMs and has a strong connection with likelihood maximization.

This paper has the following structure: in Section 2 we present a one-shot many-to-many VC model and describe DPM it relies on; Section 3 introduces a novel DPM sampling scheme and establishes its connection with likelihood maximization; the experiments regarding voice conversion task as well as those demonstrating the benefits of the proposed sampling scheme are described in Section 4; we conclude in Section 5.

## 2 VOICE CONVERSION DIFFUSION MODEL

As with many other VC models, the one we propose belongs to the family of autoencoders. In fact, any conditional DPM with data-dependent prior (i.e. terminal distribution of forward diffusion) can be seen as such: forward diffusion gradually adding Gaussian noise to data can be regarded as encoder while reverse diffusion trying to remove this noise acts as a decoder. DPMs are trained to minimize the distance (expressed in different terms for different model types) between the trajectories of forward and reverse diffusion processes thus, speaking from the perspective of autoencoders, minimizing reconstruction error. Data-dependent priors have been proposed by Popov et al. (2021) and Lee et al. (2021), and we follow the former paper due to the flexibility of the continuous DPM framework used there. Our approach is summarized in Figure 1.

## 2.1 ENCODER

We choose average phoneme-level mel features as speaker-independent speech representation. To train the encoder to convert input mel-spectrograms into those of “average voice”, we take three steps: (i) first, we apply Montreal Forced Aligner (McAuliffe et al., 2017) to a large-scale multi-speaker LibriTTS dataset (Zen et al., 2019) to align speech frames with phonemes; (ii) next, we obtain average mel features for each particular phoneme by aggregating its mel features across the whole LibriTTS dataset; (iii) the encoder is then trained to minimize mean square error between output mel-spectrograms and ground truth “average voice” mel-spectrograms (i.e. input mel-spectrograms where each phoneme mel feature is replaced with the average one calculated on the previous step).

The encoder has exactly the same Transformer-based architecture used in Grad-TTS (Popov et al., 2021) except that its inputs are mel features rather than character or phoneme embeddings. Note that unlike Grad-TTS the encoder is trained separately from the decoder described in the next section.

The described above DPM was introduced by Popov et al. (2021) for text-to-speech task and we adapt it for our purposes. We put _X_[¯] = _ϕ_ ( _X_ 0) where _ϕ_ is the encoder, i.e. _X_[¯] is the “average voice” mel-spectrogram which we want to transform into that of the target voice. We condition the decoder _sθ_ = _sθ_ ( _X_[ˆ] _t, X, g_[¯] _t_ ( _Y_ ) _, t_ ) on some trainable function _gt_ ( _Y_ ) to provide it with information about the target speaker ( _Y_ stands for forward trajectories of the target mel-spectrogram at inference and the ones of the training mel-spectrogram at training). This function is a neural network trained jointly with the decoder. We experimented with three input types for this network:
- _d-only_ – the input is the speaker embedding extracted from the target mel-spectrogram _Y_ 0 with the pre-trained speaker verification network employed in (Jia et al., 2018);

- _wodyn_ – in addition, the noisy target mel-spectrogram _Yt_ is used as input;

- _whole_ – in addition, the whole dynamics of the target mel-spectrogram under forward diffusion _{Ys|s_ = 0 _._ 5 _/_ 15 _,_ 1 _._ 5 _/_ 15 _, ..,_ 14 _._ 5 _/_ 15 _}_ is used as input.

The decoder architecture is based on U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015) and is the same as in GradTTS but with four times more channels to better capture the whole range of human voices. The speaker conditioning network _gt_ ( _Y_ ) is composed of 2D convolutions and MLPs and described in detail in Appendix H. Its output is 128-dimensional vector which is broadcast-concatenated to the concatenation of _X_[ˆ] _t_ and _X_[¯] as additional 128 channels.

## 2.3 RELATED VC MODELS

To the best of our knowledge, there exist two diffusion-based voice conversion models: VoiceGrad (Kameoka et al., 2020) and DiffSVC (Liu et al., 2021a). The one we propose differs from them in several important aspects. First, neither of the mentioned papers considers a one-shot many-tomany voice conversion scenario. Next, these models take no less than 100 reverse diffusion steps at inference while we pay special attention to reducing the number of iterations (see Section 3) achieving good quality with as few as 6 iterations. Furthermore, VoiceGrad performs voice conversion by running Langevin dynamics starting from the source mel-spectrogram, thus implicitly assuming that forward diffusion trajectories starting from the mel-spectrogram we want to synthesize are likely to pass through the neighborhood of the source mel-spectrogram on their way to Gaussian noise. Such an assumption allowing to have only one network instead of encoder-decoder architecture is too strong and hardly holds for real voices. Finally, DiffSVC performs singing voice conversion and relies on PPGs as speaker-independent speech representation.

## 3 MAXIMUM LIKELIHOOD SDE SOLVER

In this section, we develop a fixed-step first-order reverse SDE solver that maximizes the loglikelihood of sample paths of the forward diffusion. This solver differs from general-purpose Euler-Maruyama SDE solver (Kloeden & Platen, 1992) by infinitesimally small values which can however become significant when we sample from diffusion model using a few iterations.
Consider the following forward and reverse SDEs defined in Euclidean space R _[n]_ for _t ∈_ [0 _,_ 1]:

where _[−→] W_ is a forward Wiener process (i.e. its forward increments _[−→] Wt − W[−→] s_ are independent of _[−→] Ws_ for _t > s_ ) and _[←−] W_ is a backward Wiener process (i.e. backward increments _[←−] Ws − W[←−] t_ are independent of _[←−] Wt_ for _s < t_ ). Following Song et al. (2021c) we will call DPM (6) Variance Preserving (VP). For simplicity we will derive maximum likelihood solver for this particular type of diffusion models. The equation (1) underlying VC diffusion model described in Section 2 can be transformed into the equation (6-F) by a constant shift and we will call such diffusion models Mean Reverting Variance Preserving (MR-VP). VP model analysis carried out in this section can be easily extended (see Appendices D, E and F) to MR-VP model as well as to other common diffusion model types such as sub-VP and VE described by Song et al. (2021c).

Note that certain Lipschitz constraints should be satisfied by coefficients of SDEs (6) to guarantee existence of strong solutions (Liptser & Shiryaev, 1978), and throughout this section we assume these conditions are satisfied as well as those from (Anderson, 1982) which guarantee that paths _X_[ˆ] generated by the reverse SDE (6-R) for the optimal _θ[∗]_ equal forward SDE (6-F) paths _X_ in distribution.

The generative procedure of a VP DPM consists in solving the reverse SDE (6-R) backwards in time starting from _X_[ˆ] 1 _∼N_ (0 _,_ I). Common Euler-Maruyama solver introduces discretization error (Kloeden & Platen, 1992) which may harm sample quality when the number of iterations is small. At the same time, it is possible to design unbiased (Henry-Labordere et al., 2017) or even exact (Beskos` & Roberts, 2005) numerical solvers for some particular SDE types. The Theorem 1 shows that in the case of diffusion models we can make use of the forward diffusion (6-F) and propose a reverse SDE solver which is better than the general-purpose Euler-Maruyama one in terms of likelihood.

The Theorem 1 provides an improved DPM sampling scheme which comes at no additional computational cost compared to standard methods (except for data-dependent term in _σ[∗]_ as discussed in Section 4.3) and requires neither model re-training nor extensive search on noise schedule space. ## 4 EXPERIMENTS

We trained two groups of models: _Diff-VCTK_ models on VCTK (Yamagishi et al., 2019) dataset containing 109 speakers (9 speakers were held out for testing purposes) and _Diff-LibriTTS_ models on LibriTTS (Zen et al., 2019) containing approximately 1100 speakers (10 speakers were held out). For every model both encoder and decoder were trained on the same dataset. Training hyperparameters, implementation and data processing details can be found in Appendix I. For mel-spectrogram inversion, we used the pre-trained universal HiFi-GAN vocoder (Kong et al., 2020) operating at 22 _._ 05kHz. All subjective human evaluation was carried out on Amazon Mechanical Turk (AMT) with Master assessors to ensure the reliability of the obtained Mean Opinion Scores (MOS). In all AMT tests we considered unseen-to-unseen conversion with 25 unseen (for both _Diff-VCTK_ and _Diff-LibriTTS_ ) speakers: 9 VCTK speakers, 10 LibriTTS speakers and 6 internal speakers. For VCTK source speakers we also ensured that source phrases were unseen during training. We place other details of listening AMT tests in Appendix J. A small subset of speech samples used in them is available at our demo page https://diffvc-fast-ml-solver.github.io which we encourage to visit. The code will soon be published at https://github.com/huawei-noah/ Speech-Backbones.

## 4.1 SPEAKER CONDITIONING ANALYSIS

For each dataset we trained three models – one for each input type for the speaker conditioning network _gt_ ( _Y_ ) (see Section 2.2). Although these input types had much influence neither on speaker similarity nor on speech naturalness, we did two experiments to choose the best models (one for each training dataset) in terms of speaker similarity for further comparison with baseline systems. We compared voice conversion results (produced by _ML-30_ sampling scheme) on 92 source-target pairs. AMT workers were asked which of three models (if any) sounded most similar to the target speaker and which of them (if any) sounded least similar. For _Diff-VCTK_ and _Diff-LibriTTS_ models each conversion pair was evaluated 4 and 5 times respectively. Table 1 demonstrates that for both _Diff-VCTK_ and _Diff-LibriTTS_ the best option is _wodyn_ , i.e. to condition the decoder at time _t_ on the speaker embedding together with the noisy target mel-spectrogram _Yt_ . Conditioning on _Yt_ allows making use of diffusion-specific information of how the noisy target sounds whereas embedding from the pre-trained speaker verification network contains information only about the clean target. Taking these results into consideration, we used _Diff-VCTK-wodyn_ and _Diff-LibriTTS-wodyn_ in the remaining experiments.

## 4.2 ANY-TO-ANY VOICE CONVERSION

We chose four recently proposed VC models capable of one-shot many-to-many synthesis as the baselines:
- _AGAIN-VC_ (Chen et al., 2021b), an improved version of a conventional autoencoder AdaINVC solving the disentanglement problem by means of instance normalization;
- _FragmentVC_ (Lin et al., 2021), an attention-based model relying on wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020) to obtain speech content from the source utterance;

- _VQMIVC_ (Wang et al., 2021), state-of-the-art approach among those employing vector quantization techniques;
- _BNE-PPG-VC_ (Liu et al., 2021b), an improved variant of PPG-based VC models combining a bottleneck feature extractor obtained from a phoneme recognizer with a seq2seq-based synthesis module.

As shown in (Kim et al., 2021), PPG-based VC models provide high voice conversion quality competitive even with that of the state-of-the-art VC models taking text transcription corresponding to the source utterance as input. Therefore, we can consider _BNE-PPG-VC_ a state-of-the-art model in our setting.

Baseline voice conversion results were produced by the pre-trained VC models provided in official GitHub repositories. Since only _BNE-PPG-VC_ has the model pre-trained on a large-scale dataset (namely, LibriTTS + VCTK), we did two subjective human evaluation tests: the first one comparing _Diff-VCTK_ with _AGAIN-VC_ , _FragmentVC_ and _VQMIVC_ trained on VCTK and the second one comparing _Diff-LibriTTS_ with _BNE-PPG-VC_ . The results of these tests are given in Tables 2 and 3 respectively. Speech naturalness and speaker similarity were assessed separately. AMT workers evaluated voice conversion quality on 350 source-target pairs on 5-point scale. In the first test, each pair was assessed 6 times on average both in speech naturalness and speaker similarity evaluation; as for the second one, each pair was assessed 8 and 9 times on average in speech naturalness and speaker similarity evaluation correspondingly. No less than 41 unique assessors took part in each test.

Table 2 demonstrates that our model performs significantly better than the baselines both in terms of naturalness and speaker similarity even when 6 reverse diffusion iterations are used. Despite working almost equally well on VCTK speakers, the best baseline _VQMIVC_ shows poor performance on other speakers perhaps because of not being able to generalize to different domains with lower recording quality. Although _Diff-VCTK_ performance also degrades on non-VCTK speakers, it achieves good speaker similarity of MOS 3 _._ 6 on VCTK ones when _ML-30_ sampling scheme is used and only slightly worse MOS 3 _._ 5 when 5x less iterations are used at inference.

Table 3 contains human evaluation results of _Diff-LibriTTS_ for four sampling schemes: _ML-30_ with 30 reverse SDE solver steps and _ML-6_ , _EM-6_ and _PF-6_ with 6 steps of reverse diffusion. The three schemes taking 6 steps achieved real-time factor (RTF) around 0 _._ 1 on GPU (i.e. inference was 10 times faster than real time) while the one taking 30 steps had RTF around 0 _._ 5. The proposed model _Diff-LibriTTS-ML-30_ and the baseline _BNE-PPG-VC_ show the same performance on the VCTK test set in terms of speech naturalness the latter being slightly better in terms of speaker similarity which can perhaps be explained by the fact that _BNE-PPG-VC_ was trained on the union of VCTK and LibriTTS whereas our model was trained only on LibriTTS. As for the whole test set containing unseen LibriTTS and internal speakers also, _Diff-LibriTTS-ML-30_ outperforms _BNE-PPG-VC_ model achieving MOS 4 _._ 0 and 3 _._ 4 in terms of speech naturalness and speaker similarity respectively. Due to employing PPG extractor trained on a large-scale ASR dataset LibriSpeech (Panayotov et al., 2015), _BNE-PPG-VC_ has fewer mispronunciation issues than our model but synthesized speech suffers from more sonic artifacts. This observation makes us believe that incorporating PPG features in the proposed diffusion VC framework is a promising direction for future research.
Table 3 also demonstrates the benefits of the proposed maximum likelihood sampling scheme over other sampling methods for a small number of inference steps: only _ML-N_ scheme allows us to

## 4.3 MAXIMUM LIKELIHOOD SAMPLING

Figure 2: CIFAR-10 images randomly sampled from VP DPM by running 10 reverse diffusion steps with the following schemes (from left to right): “euler-maruyama”, “probability flow”, “maximum likelihood ( _τ_ = 0 _._ 5)”, “maximum likelihood ( _τ_ = 1 _._ 0)”.

To show that the maximum likelihood sampling scheme proposed in Section 3 generalizes to different tasks and DPM types, we took the models trained by Song et al. (2021c) on CIFAR-10 image generation task and compared our method with other sampling schemes described in that paper in terms of Fr´echet Inception Distance (FID).

The main difficulty in applying maximum likelihood SDE solver is estimating data-dependent term E[Tr (Var ( _X_ 0 _|Xt_ ))] in _σt,h[∗]_[.][Although in the current experiments we just set this term to zero, we] can think of two possible ways to estimate it: (i) approximate Var ( _X_ 0 _|Xt_ ) with Var ( _X_[ˆ] 0 _|X_[ˆ] _t_ = _Xt_ ): sample noisy data _Xt_ , solve reverse SDE with sufficiently small step size starting from terminal condition _X_[ˆ] _t_ = _Xt_ several times, and calculate sample variance of the resulting solutions at initial points _X_[ˆ] 0; (ii) use the formula (58) from Appendix C to calculate Var ( _X_ 0 _|Xt_ ) assuming that _X_ 0 is distributed normally with mean and variance equal to sample mean and sample variance computed on the training dataset. Experimenting with these techniques and exploring new ones seems to be an interesting future research direction.

Another important practical consideration is that the proposed scheme is proven to be optimal only for score matching networks trained till optimality. Therefore, in the experiments whose results are reported in Table 4 we apply maximum likelihood sampling scheme only when _t ≤ τ_ while using standard Euler-Maruyama solver for _t > τ_ for some hyperparameter _τ ∈_ [0 _,_ 1]. Such a modification relies on the assumption that score matching network is closer to being optimal for smaller noise.

Table 4 shows that despite likelihood and FID are two metrics that do not perfectly correlate (Song et al., 2021b), in most cases our maximum likelihood SDE solver performs best in terms of FID. Also, it is worth mentioning that although _τ_ = 1 is always rather a good choice, tuning this hyperparameter can lead to even better performance. One can find randomly chosen generated images for various sampling methods in Figure 2.

## 5 CONCLUSION

In this paper, the novel one-shot many-to-many voice conversion model has been presented. Its encoder design and powerful diffusion-based decoder made it possible to achieve good results both in terms of speaker similarity and speech naturalness even on out-of-domain unseen speakers. Subjective human evaluation verified that the proposed model delivers scalable VC solution with competitive performance. Furthermore, aiming at fast synthesis, we have developed and theoretically justified the novel sampling scheme. The main idea behind it is to modify the general-purpose Euler-Maruyama SDE solver so as to maximize the likelihood of discrete sample paths of the forward diffusion. Due to the proposed sampling scheme, our VC model is capable of high-quality voice conversion with as few as 6 reverse diffusion steps. Moreover, experiments on the image generation task show that all known diffusion model types can benefit from the proposed SDE solver.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a compelling contribution by introducing a fast maximum likelihood sampling scheme for diffusion-based voice conversion, achieving competitive results with few inference steps. The integration of a theoretically grounded solver into a practical VC system is a strength, and the ablation across sampling methods and conditioning strategies adds depth. The model performs well on subjective metrics, particularly in large-scale settings.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient comparison with recent diffusion-based VC baselines: The paper compares against several non-diffusion one-shot VC models (e.g., AGAIN-VC, FragmentVC) but omits comparisons with more relevant and recent diffusion-based approaches such as FastVoiceGrad (2024), which achieves one-step voice conversion, or LatentVoiceGrad (2025), which improves scalability and quality in latent space. Given that the paper's main technical novelty lies in fast sampling for diffusion models, failing to benchmark against these direct competitors undermines the claim of state-of-the-art performance in the diffusion VC space. Readers will wonder whether the proposed 6-step ML solver truly advances the field or is outperformed by newer single-step methods. The authors should include at least one recent diffusion-based baseline in the MOS evaluation or justify its absence with a discussion of architectural incompatibilities.

- Overstated generalization claims without cross-dataset or zero-shot robustness analysis: The paper claims strong performance on 'unseen-to-unseen' speakers, including internal and LibriTTS speakers, but provides no evidence of cross-dataset generalization or robustness to domain shift. For instance, it is unclear how the model performs when the reference utterance is noisy, in a different language, or from a speaker with strong accent—common challenges in real-world deployment. The evaluation is limited to clean, in-domain speech from VCTK and LibriTTS, which weakens the 'scalable' and 'any-to-any' claims. A natural question is whether the average-voice encoder and diffusion decoder truly generalize beyond the training distribution. The authors should include a controlled ablation on out-of-distribution samples or at least acknowledge this limitation in the conclusion.

- Inadequate justification for skipping the variance term in the ML solver: The maximum likelihood solver (Theorem 1) includes a data-dependent variance term $\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{Tr}(\mathrm{Var}(X_0|X_t))]$ in $\sigma_{t,h}^{*}$, which the authors omit in practice, arguing it is 'rather small because of strong conditioning.' However, no empirical validation is provided—no ablation shows the impact of this approximation on sample quality or diversity. In high-dimensional mel-spectrogram generation, even small errors in variance estimation can accumulate and degrade temporal coherence or speaker fidelity. This omission risks undermining the theoretical justification of the solver, especially since the improvement over Euler-Maruyama is most pronounced at low step counts where approximation errors matter most. The authors should either include an ablation with estimated variance (e.g., via reverse sampling) or weaken the theoretical claims to match the practical implementation.

- Lack of objective metrics correlating with perceptual quality: The evaluation relies heavily on MOS for naturalness and similarity, but includes no objective measures such as speaker verification scores (e.g., cosine similarity in embedding space), reconstruction error (e.g., mel-cepstral distortion), or alignment scores (e.g., DTW distance between source and converted speech). While MOS is important, the absence of objective correlates makes it difficult to interpret whether quality improvements are due to better speaker identity transfer, content preservation, or reduced artifacts. For example, Table 1 uses a speaker similarity percentage but does not define how it was computed or validated. Including at least one standard objective metric would strengthen the validity of the human evaluation and allow for broader reproducibility.

- Ambiguous speaker conditioning setup in inference: The paper states that the decoder is conditioned on $g_t(Y)$, where $Y$ is the forward diffusion trajectory of the target mel-spectrogram. However, in inference, the target mel-spectrogram is not observed—only a single reference utterance is available. It is unclear whether $Y_t$ is computed by applying forward diffusion to the mel-spectrogram of the reference utterance, and if so, how this affects robustness to reference quality. This is critical because the 'wodyn' and 'whole' conditioning schemes depend on noisy or full-trajectory versions of $Y$, which are not available at test time unless explicitly generated. The method section should clarify the exact inference-time procedure for constructing $Y_t$ and discuss potential sensitivity to reference utterance length or noise.

- No ablation of variance term approximation in ML solver: The paper claims theoretical justification for the maximum likelihood SDE solver but omits the data-dependent variance term $\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{Tr}(\mathrm{Var}(X_0|X_t))]$ in $\sigma_{t,h}^{*}$ during experiments, asserting it is small due to strong conditioning. However, no ablation study shows the effect of this approximation on sample quality, diversity, or speaker fidelity—especially critical at 6-step generation where errors accumulate. Readers will wonder whether the performance gain comes from the proposed scheme or simply from other factors like conditioning design. To validate the claim, the authors should compare ML-6 sampling with and without an estimated version of this term—e.g., using reverse sampling from $X_t$ to approximate $\mathrm{Var}(\hat{X}_0|\hat{X}_t = X_t)$ on a held-out set—and report MOS or FID differences.

- Missing objective metrics for speaker similarity and content preservation: The evaluation relies entirely on subjective MOS for naturalness and similarity, with no objective correlates such as speaker embedding cosine similarity, mel-cepstral distortion, or phonetic alignment scores. This makes it hard to interpret whether quality improvements stem from better identity transfer, content retention, or artifact reduction. For instance, Table 1 reports 'most similar' percentages but does not specify how similarity was measured or verified. A standard practice in voice conversion is to report automated speaker verification scores (e.g., using ECAPA-TDNN) on converted speech. The authors should compute such metrics on their test set and include them alongside MOS to strengthen reproducibility and support their claims.

- Unverified generalization of sampling scheme to image models: The paper claims the proposed solver generalizes to image generation and evaluates it on CIFAR-10 using FID, but provides no evidence that the same hyperparameters (e.g., $\tau = 0.5$) or implementation choices (e.g., skipping the variance term) are appropriate for images. Image and mel-spectrogram data differ significantly in structure, conditioning, and score network training dynamics. A natural question is whether the solver's gains hold under fair comparison with tuned baselines. The authors should either retrain or fine-tune the CIFAR-10 score network under the same assumptions, or at minimum include a comparison with DDIM or DPM-Solver at matching step counts to contextualize the FID results.

- No comparison with recent fast diffusion VC baselines: The paper positions its 6-step sampling as a major efficiency advance but omits comparison with newer diffusion-based VC models like FastVoiceGrad or LatentVoiceGrad, which achieve high quality in one or few steps. Since the core contribution is speed without retraining, failing to benchmark against these direct competitors weakens the novelty claim. Readers will question whether the proposed method meaningfully advances the state of the art. The authors should include at least one recent diffusion-based baseline in the MOS or FID evaluation, or explicitly discuss architectural barriers that prevent such comparison—e.g., differences in conditioning or latent space design.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Include comparisons with at least two recent diffusion-based VC models (e.g., FastVoiceGrad, LatentVoiceGrad) in the subjective evaluation or provide a detailed justification for their exclusion.

- Conduct an ablation or analysis demonstrating the effect of omitting the conditional variance term in the ML solver, either through approximation or sensitivity studies.

- Add objective evaluation metrics such as speaker similarity embedding distance, mel-cepstral distortion, or alignment error to complement MOS scores.

- Clarify the inference-time construction of the target diffusion trajectory $Y_t$ and discuss implications for real-world use.

- Strengthen claims of generalization by either testing on out-of-domain samples or explicitly limiting the scope to in-distribution unseen speakers.

comments.title: Omission of variance term invalidates theoretical optimality claim
comments.quote: $(\sigma_{t,h}^{*})^{2}=\sigma_{t-h,t}^{2}+\frac{1}{n}\nu_{t-h,t}^{2}\mathbb{E}_{X_{t}}\left[\mathrm{Tr}\left(\mathrm{Var}\left(X_{0}|X_{t}\right)\right)\right],$
comments.explanation: The theoretical justification in Theorem 1 relies on the full expression of $\sigma_{t,h}^{*}$, including the data-dependent variance term $\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{Tr}(\mathrm{Var}(X_0|X_t))]$. However, the paper explicitly omits this term in practice, stating it is 'rather small because of strong conditioning' without empirical validation. This undermines the claim that the implemented solver is 'maximum likelihood'. The theoretical optimality no longer applies to the actual method used in experiments. Either provide ablation results showing the term's impact is negligible, or revise the claim to acknowledge that the practical solver is an approximation that deviates from the theoretically justified version.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of $Y$ creates inference ambiguity
comments.quote: Figure 1: VC model training and inference.  $Y$  stands for the training mel-spectrogram at training and the target mel-spectrogram at inference. Speaker conditioning in the decoder is enabled by the speaker conditioning network  $g_{t}(Y)$  where  $Y = \{Y_{t}\}_{t\in [0,1]}$  is the whole forward diffusion trajectory starting at  $Y_{0}$ .
comments.explanation: The symbol $Y$ is used to denote both a single mel-spectrogram ($Y_0$) and its full forward diffusion trajectory ($\{Y_t\}_{t\in[0,1]}$), creating ambiguity. During inference, only $Y_0$ is available; the trajectory $Y_t$ must be generated by applying forward diffusion. The paper does not clarify whether $g_t(Y)$ uses a precomputed trajectory from $Y_0$ or depends on stochastic noise realizations. This omission makes the method underspecified for real-world deployment. The conditioning mechanism must be explicitly defined for inference, including how $Y_t$ is synthesized from the reference utterance.

comments.title: Misleading claim of out-of-domain generalization
comments.quote: Its encoder design and powerful diffusion-based decoder made it possible to achieve good results both in terms of speaker similarity and speech naturalness even on out-of-domain unseen speakers.
comments.explanation: The term 'out-of-domain unseen speakers' suggests robustness to distribution shift (e.g., noise, accent, language), but the evaluation is limited to clean English speech from VCTK and LibriTTS—datasets similar in domain and quality to training data. No experiments test performance under true domain shift. The results support generalization to *unseen* speakers, not *out-of-domain* ones. This overstatement misrepresents the model's capabilities. The sentence should be revised to 'even on unseen speakers from in-domain datasets' to accurately reflect the evaluation scope.

comments.title: FID results for VE DPM with Probability Flow appear erroneous
comments.explanation: The FID value of 214.5 for VE DPM with Probability Flow at $N=100$ is inconsistent with the trend of decreasing FID at higher step counts and with the performance of other methods (e.g., Euler-Maruyama achieves 24.77). Probability Flow, as a deterministic ODE solver, typically outperforms ancestral sampling and should not degrade at higher step counts. This outlier suggests a possible reporting error or implementation issue. The value should be verified against original logs, as an unexplained anomaly in benchmarking undermines confidence in the comparative results.

comments.title: Overstated generality of sampling scheme
comments.quote: Moreover, experiments on the image generation task show that all known diffusion model types can benefit from the proposed SDE solver.
comments.explanation: The claim that 'all known diffusion model types' benefit from the solver is not supported by the evidence. Experiments are limited to CIFAR-10 with a single model type (VP DPM), with no testing on latent diffusion, score-based models with non-standard schedules, or high-resolution image generators. The paper does not establish broad applicability. This overgeneralization risks misleading readers. The sentence should be revised to reflect the actual scope: 'suggest that the proposed SDE solver can benefit certain diffusion model types under standard settings.'

comments.title: Unjustified assumption about score network optimality at low noise
comments.quote: Such a modification relies on the assumption that score matching network is closer to being optimal for smaller noise.
comments.explanation: The paper assumes the score network is more accurate at low noise levels ($t \approx 0$) to justify applying the ML solver only in that regime. However, in practice, score estimation is often harder at low noise due to sharper gradients and fine-detail modeling. No evidence—such as score error norms or consistency checks—is provided to support this assumption. The rationale for the hybrid sampling scheme ($t \leq \tau$) is therefore heuristic, not grounded in model behavior. The claim should be rephrased to acknowledge it as a practical choice rather than a justified property of training convergence.

comments.title: Conflation of likelihood optimality with FID improvement
comments.quote: Table 4 shows that despite likelihood and FID are two metrics that do not perfectly correlate (Song et al., 2021b), in most cases our maximum likelihood SDE solver performs best in terms of FID.
comments.explanation: The paper uses FID improvement to validate the maximum likelihood solver, but this conflates distinct objectives. Theorem 1 establishes optimality in path likelihood, not perceptual quality. Prior work shows likelihood and FID can be inversely related. Presenting FID gains as evidence for the theoretical framework risks implying a stronger connection than exists. The best $\tau$ is determined empirically, suggesting performance depends on tuning, not theoretical properties. The discussion should clarify that FID benefits are empirical observations, not direct consequences of likelihood maximization.

comments.title: Ambiguity in construction of 'whole' conditioning input
comments.quote: whole – in addition, the whole dynamics of the target mel-spectrogram under forward diffusion $\{Y_{s}|s=0.5/15,1.5/15,..,14.5/15\}$ is used as input.
comments.explanation: The 'whole' conditioning uses 15 forward-diffused mel-spectrograms $Y_s$, but the paper does not specify how these are generated from $Y_0$ at inference. Since the forward process is stochastic, each $Y_s$ depends on a noise realization, making $g_t(Y)$ non-deterministic unless noise is fixed or averaged. The input format (e.g., concatenated, stacked) is also not described. This lack of detail affects reproducibility. The method must specify the noise sampling strategy and input structure to ensure consistent and deterministic behavior.

comments.title: Inconsistent AMT evaluation counts affect model selection
comments.quote: For Diff-VCTK and Diff-LibriTTS models each conversion pair was evaluated 4 and 5 times respectively. Table 1 demonstrates that for both Diff-VCTK and Diff-LibriTTS the best option is wodyn, i.e. to condition the decoder at time  $t$  on the speaker embedding together with the noisy target mel-spectrogram  $Y_{t}$ .
comments.explanation: The selection of 'wodyn' as the best conditioning scheme is based on different numbers of AMT judgments per dataset (4 vs 5), introducing an imbalance in statistical reliability. This inconsistency could bias the outcome, especially given the close scores in Table 1. Since this choice determines the models used in all downstream experiments, the decision should rest on a consistent evaluation protocol. The paper should either equalize the number of evaluations or apply statistical weighting and report confidence intervals for the preference rates.

comments.title: Typographical error in Euler-Maruyama noise term
comments.quote: We also experimented with Euler-Maruyama solver  $EM-N$  (i.e.  $\hat{\kappa}_{t,h} = 0$ ,  $\hat{\omega}_{t,h} = 0$ ,  $\hat{\sigma}_{t,h} = \sqrt{\beta_t\hbar}$ )
comments.explanation: The Euler-Maruyama solver is defined with $\hat{\sigma}_{t,h} = \sqrt{\beta_t\hbar}$, but $\hbar$ is undefined and likely a typo for $h$, the step size. Using $\hbar$—a symbol from quantum mechanics—introduces confusion and breaks consistency with standard SDE notation. The correct term should be $\sqrt{\beta_t h}$, as used in equation (12). This error, while probably not affecting implementation, undermines clarity and precision in a critical part of the method description.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper presents a diffusion-based voice conversion system with a novel maximum likelihood sampling scheme for fast inference. While the approach shows promising results in subjective evaluations, there are significant concerns regarding the validation of the fast sampling claims, the adequacy of baseline comparisons, and the generalization capabilities of the system.

The paper makes a solid contribution by adapting diffusion models to one-shot voice conversion and proposing a theoretically motivated fast sampling scheme. The experimental setup is comprehensive with multiple datasets and subjective evaluations. However, the claims about computational efficiency and generalization are not sufficiently supported by the presented evidence.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient validation of computational efficiency claims: The paper claims the proposed sampling scheme enables real-time voice conversion with only 6 iterations, but provides only limited evidence. Table 3 mentions RTF around 0.1 for 6-step methods but doesn't compare this to baselines' computational costs. More critically, the paper doesn't measure actual end-to-end inference time including the encoder and vocoder components. A proper computational efficiency analysis should include: (1) detailed runtime measurements comparing all components of the pipeline, (2) memory usage comparisons, and (3) analysis of how the fast sampling affects quality-efficiency trade-offs. Without these measurements, the real-time applicability claims remain unsubstantiated.

- Inadequate comparison with recent diffusion-based VC methods: The paper compares against GAN-based and PPG-based baselines but lacks comparison with contemporary diffusion-based voice conversion methods. While Section 2.3 mentions VoiceGrad and DiffSVC, these are not included in the experimental comparisons. Given the rapid progress in diffusion models for voice conversion (as evidenced by recent works like DiffVC+, Diff-HierVC, and FastVoiceGrad in the literature context), the paper should compare against these state-of-the-art diffusion approaches. The current comparisons don't adequately demonstrate whether the proposed advances are due to the diffusion framework itself or the specific innovations in this work.

- Unverified assumptions about speaker disentanglement: The paper assumes the encoder trained to produce 'average voice' features successfully disentangles speaker identity from content, but provides limited evidence for this claim. While the subjective speaker similarity scores are reasonable (Tables 2-3), there's no analysis of potential voice leakage or content preservation. The paper should include: (1) objective metrics measuring speaker identity preservation (e.g., speaker verification EER), (2) analysis of content preservation (e.g., word error rate or phoneme accuracy), and (3) ablation studies showing the impact of the average voice training approach. Without these analyses, the effectiveness of the proposed disentanglement method remains uncertain.

- Limited evaluation of generalization capabilities: The paper claims the model works for 'any-to-any' voice conversion but only evaluates on English speakers from VCTK and LibriTTS datasets. There's no testing on cross-lingual scenarios, different acoustic environments, or expressive speech. The literature context highlights robustness to out-of-domain speakers as a key challenge, but the paper doesn't address this. The evaluation should include: (1) cross-lingual voice conversion tests, (2) performance on noisy or reverberant conditions, (3) evaluation with emotional or expressive speech, and (4) analysis of failure cases. Without these tests, the generalization claims are not fully supported.

- Incomplete implementation details for reproducibility: The paper mentions that code will be published but lacks sufficient implementation details for reproducibility. Key missing details include: (1) the exact architecture of the speaker conditioning network (only briefly mentioned in Appendix H), (2) training hyperparameters and convergence criteria, (3) details of the Montreal Forced Aligner usage for average voice computation, and (4) the specific noise schedule parameters used. While some details are deferred to appendices, the main paper should include the most critical implementation details to ensure the work can be reproduced and built upon.

- Missing demonstration of non-vacuous theoretical result: The paper's main theoretical contribution (Theorem 1) claims to provide a maximum likelihood optimal SDE solver, but lacks concrete demonstration that this result has bite. The theorem establishes optimality under specific assumptions (e.g., score matching network trained to optimality), but the paper never shows a case where the proposed solver significantly outperforms standard methods in a controlled setting. The authors should include a synthetic experiment where the ground truth score function is known (e.g., a simple Gaussian mixture model) to demonstrate that their solver achieves higher likelihood than Euler-Maruyama when the assumptions hold. This would validate the theoretical claims before applying the method to complex real-world models.

- No worked example of average voice computation: The paper's core innovation of using an 'average voice' encoder is described at a high level but lacks a concrete worked example. While Section 2.1 outlines the three-step process, readers cannot verify whether this approach actually produces speaker-independent representations. The authors should compute the average mel features for a specific phoneme (e.g., /a/) using LibriTTS data, show the before/after transformation on example utterances from different speakers, and demonstrate quantitatively that the resulting features are indeed speaker-independent. This would help readers understand the mechanism and build intuition about why this approach works better than traditional disentanglement methods.

- Underdeveloped cross-lingual generalization claims: The abstract and introduction position this as a general any-to-any voice conversion solution, but the experiments only cover English speakers from VCTK and LibriTTS. The paper claims good results on 'out-of-domain unseen speakers' but never tests cross-lingual scenarios, which is a key challenge for one-shot VC systems. The authors should include at least one cross-lingual experiment (e.g., converting English to Mandarin or vice versa) to demonstrate whether the average voice approach generalizes beyond English phonetics. Without this, the claim of 'any-to-any' capability remains unsubstantiated for the most challenging use case.

- Missing practical guidance for variance estimation: Section 4.3 acknowledges that estimating the data-dependent term in σ* is difficult but provides only speculative solutions. The paper skips this term entirely in experiments, which undermines the practical utility of the theoretically optimal solver. The authors should implement and evaluate at least one of their proposed estimation methods (e.g., sampling-based approximation) and quantify how much performance is lost by ignoring this term. This would provide practitioners with concrete guidance on when the simplified ML-N solver is sufficient versus when full estimation is necessary.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add comprehensive computational efficiency analysis comparing end-to-end inference times against strong baselines

- Include comparisons with recent diffusion-based voice conversion methods (VoiceGrad, DiffSVC, etc.)

- Provide objective metrics for speaker disentanglement quality and content preservation

- Expand evaluation to include cross-lingual and out-of-domain scenarios

- Release complete implementation details and code to ensure reproducibility

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in decoder conditioning
comments.quote: the decoder $s_{\theta}=s_{\theta}(\bar{X}_{t},\bar{X},g_{t}(Y),t)$ on some trainable function $g_{t}(Y)$ to provide it with information about the target speaker ($Y$ stands for forward trajectories of the target mel-spectrogram at inference and the ones of the training mel-spectrogram at training).
comments.explanation: There is a critical inconsistency between the decoder definition in the text and the actual SDE equations. The text defines the decoder as $s_{\theta}(\bar{X}_{t},\bar{X},g_{t}(Y),t)$ with speaker conditioning, but equation (2) uses $s_{\theta}(\hat{X}_{t},\bar{X},t)$ without the $g_{t}(Y)$ term. This creates a mathematical inconsistency where the claimed speaker conditioning mechanism doesn't appear in the governing equations. Equation (2) should be rewritten as $d\hat{X}_{t}=\left(\frac{1}{2}(\bar{X}-\hat{X}_{t})-s_{\theta}(\hat{X}_{t},\bar{X},g_{t}(Y),t)\right)\beta_{t}dt+\sqrt{\beta_{t}}d\overleftarrow{W_{t}}$ to match the claimed architecture.

comments.title: Contradiction between theoretical optimality and experimental implementation
comments.quote: The main difficulty in applying maximum likelihood SDE solver is estimating data-dependent term  $\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{Tr}(\mathrm{Var}(X_0|X_t))]$  in  $\sigma_{t,h}^{*}$ . Although in the current experiments we just set this term to zero, we can think of two possible ways to estimate it
comments.explanation: The paper presents Theorem 1 claiming optimality of the ML SDE solver, but then admits to setting a key data-dependent term to zero in experiments. This creates a contradiction: if the theoretically optimal term is ignored, the solver is no longer optimal as claimed. The authors should either implement one of their proposed estimation methods and show the impact of including vs. ignoring this term, or clarify that their experiments use a simplified 'ML-N' variant and rename it accordingly. Currently, the claim of optimality in Theorem 1 is undermined by the experimental implementation.

comments.title: Missing baseline comparisons in large-scale evaluation
